data.title: Wall Street is lower as a U.S.-Iran truce remains uncertain, Warsh faces confirmation hearing
data.description:
U.S. stocks were in red territory on Tuesday as investors hoped for a peace deal between Iran and the U.S., while former Fed Governor Kevin Warsh started his confirmation hearings in the Senate.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.3%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.3%, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.2%.

Over in the bond market, yields were higher. The benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 5 basis points higher at 4.30%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 6 basis points to 3.79%.

U.S. President Donald Trump told CNBC that he expects the U.S. to “end up with a great deal” with Iran to end the war. He also said he does not expect to extend the ongoing ceasefire, which will expire on Wednesday. “I think they have no choice,” he said.

Vice President JD Vance, along with the U.S. delegation, is expected to land in Islamabad, Pakistan, on Wednesday for another round of talks, CNN reported.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were higher at $90, while Brent (CO1:COM) also rose to $98 per barrel.

Kevin Warsh, the former Fed governor whom President Donald Trump nominated as Federal Reserve chair, faced a barrage of questions on Tuesday during his confirmation hearings in the Senate. The confirmation hearing started at 10 a.m. ET.

Warsh accused the Fed of "missing the mark" when it failed to rein in inflation after COVID-19 in 2021 and 2022. "We need fundamental policy reforms to fix it," he said.

On the U.S. economy, retail sales jumped more than expected in March.

Pending home sales jumped 1.5% month-over-month in March, higher than consensus, the National Association of Realtors said. Moreover, February business inventories rose +0.4% MoM to $2,686.8B vs. +0.2% consensus, according to the U.S. Census Bureau.

data.title: D.R. Horton expects $33.5B-$34.5B of fiscal 2026 revenue while projecting at least $3B of operating cash flow
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: D.R. Horton (DHI) Q2 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • "The D.R. Horton team delivered solid second quarter results with consolidated pretax income of $867 million on $7.6 billion of revenues and a pretax profit margin of 11.5%." (President, CEO & Director Paul Romanowski)
  • "Our sales incentives increased during the second quarter, and we expect incentives to remain elevated for the rest of the year with a level dependent on demand, mortgage interest rates and other market conditions." (President, CEO & Director Romanowski)
  • "Earnings for the second quarter of fiscal 2026 were $2.24 per diluted share compared to $2.58 per share in the prior year quarter." (Executive VP & COO Michael Murray)
  • "Net sales orders increased 11% year-over-year in the second quarter to 24,992 homes, while total order value increased 10% to $9.2 billion, in line with our business plan and expectations." (Executive VP & CFO Bill Wheat)

Outlook

  • "Looking ahead to the third quarter, we currently expect consolidated revenues to be in the range of $8.8 billion to $9.3 billion, with homes closed by our homebuilding operations to be in the range of 23,500 to 24,000 homes." (Senior VP of Communications & People and Head of Investor Relations Jessica Hansen)
  • "For the full year of fiscal 2026, we now expect consolidated revenues of approximately $33.5 billion to $34.5 billion and homes closed by our homebuilding operations of 86,000 to 87,500 homes." (Senior VP Hansen)
  • "We continue to forecast an income tax rate for fiscal 2026 of approximately 24.5%, operating cash flow of at least $3 billion, common stock repurchases of approximately $2.5 billion and dividend payments of around $500 million." (Senior VP Hansen)
  • "We lowered our closings guide by 500." (Senior VP Hansen)

Financial Results

  • "Our gross profit margin on home sales revenue in the second quarter was 20.1%, which included a 40 basis point benefit from a favorable litigation outcome and lower-than-normal warranty costs." (Senior VP Hansen)
  • "Home sales revenues in the second quarter totaled $7 billion on 19,486 homes closed" and "Our average closing price was $361,600, down 1% sequentially and down 3% year-over-year." (Executive VP & COO Murray)
  • "During the second quarter, we repurchased 6 million shares of common stock for $904 million, reducing our outstanding share count by 8% compared to a year ago." (Executive VP & CFO Wheat)
  • "In the second quarter, our rental operations generated $12 million of pretax income on $212 million of revenues" and "Pretax income for the second quarter was $52 million on $193 million of revenues, resulting in a pretax profit margin of 26.8%." (President, CEO & Director Romanowski)

Q&A

  • Alan Ratner, Zelman & Associates LLC: "I would love to drill in a little bit more on the gross margin outlook." Executive VP & CFO Wheat: "we expect to see some incremental benefits in Q3 and Q4" and "Right now, we have nothing tangible to report in terms of anticipating inflation" from oil.
  • John Lovallo, UBS: "how would you sort of characterize demand in March relative to normal seasonality?" Executive VP & COO Murray: "We saw sales in line with normal seasonality" and "we're pleased with our sales results through mid-April."
  • Stephen Kim, Evercore ISI: "could you give us a sense for overall, what incentives may, in aggregate, represent" as a percent? President, CEO & Director Romanowski: "the incentives as a percent of revs is roughly 10%."
  • Trevor Allinson, Wolfe Research: "Have you seen the industry overall also start to make some progress on working down inventory?" President, CEO & Director Romanowski: "I think we have seen inventory levels reduce across the competitive environment."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive, with repeated acknowledgement of execution in a difficult demand backdrop (e.g., "strong results here in a tough market" (Stephen Kim, Evercore ISI)).
  • Management: neutral-to-slightly positive, emphasizing discipline and conditionality around demand and rates (e.g., "we expect incentives to remain elevated" (President, CEO & Director Romanowski) and "we will continue to adjust to market conditions with discipline" (President, CEO & Director Romanowski)).
  • Versus last quarter: the prepared tone stayed consistent on affordability and incentives; Q2 Q&A added more focus on oil/material inflation timing and on rental-policy uncertainty.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • "Net sales orders increased 11%" in Q2 vs. "net sales orders increased 3%" in Q1. (Executive VP & CFO Wheat; Executive VP & CFO Wheat)
  • Q2 inventory commentary emphasized faster turns and fewer specs: "Our completed unsold homes are down 25% from December and 35% from a year ago" vs. Q1: "7,300 of our unsold homes at quarter end were completed." (President, CEO & Director Romanowski)
  • Full-year guide language tightened on the high end: Q2: "$33.5 billion to $34.5 billion" and "86,000 to 87,500" vs. Q1: "$33.5 billion to $35 billion" and "86,000 to 88,000." (Senior VP Hansen)

Risks and Concerns

  • "New home demand remains impacted by affordability constraints and cautious consumer sentiment." (President, CEO & Director Romanowski)
  • On build-for-rent policy uncertainty: "there is uncertainty around the legislation" and "a little bit of a pause" in activity. (President, CEO & Director Romanowski)
  • On cost risk: "If we were to see an extended period where oil prices stayed elevated for an extended period, then there could be some pressure." (Executive VP & CFO Wheat)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 as a quarter where sales and margins held up better than expected amid affordability pressure, while reiterating that incentives should remain elevated and results will stay rate- and demand-dependent. Guidance for fiscal 2026 was narrowed on the high end, with the company continuing to target at least $3 billion in operating cash flow and approximately $2.5 billion in buybacks, alongside ongoing efforts to improve cycle times, reduce completed inventory, and protect returns through disciplined starts and lot underwriting.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Halliburton rises after Q1 earnings topper; sees oil recovery coming in North America
data.description:
Halliburton (HAL) up 4% in Tuesday's trading after reporting better-than-expected Q1 adjusted earnings, as strong revenue growth in Latin America helped offset weaker sales due to the Middle East war.

The company's Q1 revenue for Latin America jumped 22% Y/Y to $1.1B, driven by higher activity in Ecuador, the Caribbean, and Brazil, and revenue for Europe and Africa rose 11% to $858M.

Steady demand in those regions helped offset a 13% decline in revenue for the Middle East and Asia, as attacks on energy infrastructure in countries such as Saudi Arabia and Qatar have dragged down crude production levels across the region.

The war reduced Q1 net income in Halliburton's (HAL) drilling and evaluation divisions by $0.02-$0.03/share, with impacts in the $0.07-$0.09 range for Q2, CFO Eric Carre said on the company's earnings conference call.

Q1 revenue in North America fell 4.5% to $2.14B, but CEO Jeff Miller said the company is in the "early innings of a recovery."

"We are increasingly bullish ⁠on ​the [North America] recovery as the back end of the ​oil curve picks up to $80-plus," Barclays analysts said in a note.

Halliburton (HAL) "posted a solid beat across the board... driven by international strength that more than offset continued North America softness," Melus Research analyst James West said in a note.

Halliburton (HAL) is the first major oilfield services contractor to report results since the start of the war, which has been a blow to the sector that looks to the Middle East for growth as shale fields in the U.S. mature.

data.title: IBM Q1 2026 Earnings Preview
data.description:
IBM (IBM) is scheduled to announce Q1 earnings results on Wednesday, April 22nd, after market close.

Shares have dropped around 13.5% YTD, with investors cautious about AI disruptions. However, analysts appear largely positive of IBM’s Q1 results.

“We believe IBM is set up for a software beat in 1Q following a mixed cadence over the past year (beat in 4Q, miss in 2Q, in line in 1Q/3Q),” Jefferies analyst Brent Thill wrote in a note to clients, citing in part an earlier-than-expected close of the Confluent (CFLT) acquisition (mid-March vs June expectations), and growth from Red Hat.

"While the prior year ended with a broad-based beat in Q4, the software outperformance came despite slower than expected growth from Hybrid Cloud, aka Red Hat," said RBC Capital Markets analysts, led by Matthew Swanson, in an investor note. "We'd expect things to pick up where they left off with a top-line beat driven by infrastructure and software strength being driven by Automation and Transaction Processing."

"Recent announcements continue to reinforce IBM's AI narrative heading into the print," said Wedbush analysts, led by Dan Ives, in a Friday investor note.

Investors will be closely watching management comments on the Confluent acquisition, as well as quantum computing initiatives, AI infrastructure and automation and software growth prospects.

The consensus EPS Estimate is $1.81 (+13.1% Y/Y) and the consensus Revenue Estimate is $15.65B (+7.6% Y/Y).

Over the last 2 years, IBM has beaten EPS estimates 100% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 75% of the time.

Over the last 3 months, EPS estimates have seen 4 upward revisions and 9 downward. Revenue estimates have seen 10 upward revisions and 2 downward.

data.title: Pentagon details Trump’s $1.5T defense plan; focus on missiles, drones, ships
data.description:
The Pentagon on Tuesday released additional details of President Donald Trump’s proposed $1.5 trillion defense budget for fiscal 2027, a request that would mark the biggest annual increase in military spending since World War II if approved by Congress.

The proposal combines a base request of $1.15 trillion with a separate $350 billion supplemental package that would require additional legislative approval, similar to the budget process used last year.

Defense officials said the plan introduces a new spending category labeled “presidential priorities,” which includes Golden Dome missile defense, artificial intelligence, data systems, drone programs and investments aimed at strengthening the U.S. defense industrial base, Reuters reported.

Shipbuilding push takes center stage

The budget allocates more than $65 billion for ship procurement, including 18 warships and 16 support vessels to be built by companies such as General Dynamics (GD) and Huntington Ingalls Industries (HII). Officials described the effort, branded the “Golden Fleet” initiative, as the largest shipbuilding request since 1962.

Aircraft procurement climbs

The proposal would increase purchases of Lockheed Martin’s (LMT) F-35 fighter to 85 aircraft annually. It also sets aside $102 billion for aircraft procurement, research and development, representing a 26% increase from the prior year.

Next-generation aviation systems remain a priority, including Boeing’s (BA) F-47 fighter program. The budget also seeks $6.1 billion for Northrop Grumman’s (NOC) B-21 bomber.

Record spending on drone warfare

Senior officials described the request as the largest U.S. investment to date in drone warfare and counter-drone systems. The plan includes $53.6 billion for autonomous platforms and battlefield logistics, along with $21 billion for munitions, anti-drone defenses and other advanced systems.

Funding for the Defense Autonomous Warfare Group would surge to roughly $54 billion from about $225 million previously. Officials said most of that spending would go toward deploying existing technology rather than early-stage research. The office has also absorbed the Pentagon’s earlier Replicator drone initiative.

Troop pay and force expansion

The budget includes pay raises weighted toward lower ranks, with junior enlisted personnel set for a 7% increase, more senior enlisted troops receiving 6% and top ranks getting 5%.

The Pentagon also wants to expand the military by 44,000 service members in fiscal 2027, after adding more than 20,000 in fiscal 2026.

Iran costs left for later request

The proposal does not include funding tied to the conflict with Iran. A senior Pentagon official said a separate supplemental request will likely be needed later to cover near-term operations and replenish military stockpiles, Reuters reported.

The plan now heads to Congress, where lawmakers will weigh both the size of the increase and the administration’s priorities across missiles, ships, aircraft and autonomous systems.

data.title: First Commonwealth Financial dividend preview: April raise expected to extend 9-year run
data.description:
First Commonwealth Financial (FCF) is expected to announce a dividend increase this April, continuing its 9-year streak of regular dividend growth.

Based on historical data, analysts expect a consensus annual dividend of about ~$0.56 per share. This equals a quarterly dividend of ~$0.14 per share, which implies an increase of ~3.7% from the current ~$0.1350 per share payout.

The company last paid a dividend of ~$0.1350 per share in January 2026, which comes to an annual yield of 2.87%.

Earlier, in April last year, it increased the dividend by ~3.8%, from ~$0.13 to ~$0.1350.

Over the past five years, the company has delivered a dividend growth rate of ~4.18% and currently maintains a payout ratio of ~35.53%. 

Meanwhile, on dividend quality metrics, the company carries ratings of D+ for safety, D+ for growth, C+ for yield, and A- for consistency.

First Commonwealth Financial (FCF) is set to report its Q1 2026 financial results on April 28, 2026, before the market opens.

data.title: OpenAI adds cost-per-click ads within ChatGPT: report
data.description:
OpenAI (OPENAI) has turned on cost-per-click ads within ChatGPT as it continues to turn on revenue streams ahead of its anticipated initial public offering, according to Digiday.

Unlike cost-per-mille ads, which generate revenue for every 1,000 impressions, CPC ads create revenue per click. In OpenAI's case, the bids range from $3 to $5 per click, according to the report. ChatGPT started CPMs about two and a half months ago, but the price charged per 1,000 impressions has declined from $60 to as low as $25.

CPCs are a model that has been perfected over the years by Google (GOOG)(GOOGL), the report said. It provides a range of metrics that can affect advertisers' bids, such as intent signals, quality scores, retargeting data, and auction pressure.

In addition to the CPCs, OpenAI has also posted a job opening for an Advertising Marketing Science Lead position. The role is extensive. It requires one to define OpenAI's advertiser measurement strategy, build the foundation of OpenAI's marketing science function, lead advertiser-facing measurement discussions, develop clear advertiser narratives, design and govern OpenAI's advertising measurement program, build scalable measurement frameworks, establish privacy-centric measurement approaches, translate measurement strategies into product capabilities, and share OpenAI's external measurement system.

Seeking Alpha reached out to OpenAI for comment on these developments.

data.title: IBM seen 'picking up speed' as Q1 results await: Jefferies
data.description:
• IBM (IBM) appears to be “picking up speed” ahead of its first-quarter results, set to be released later this week, Jefferies said. 
  • “We believe IBM is set up for a software beat in 1Q following a mixed cadence over the past year (beat in 4Q, miss in 2Q, in line in 1Q/3Q),” Jefferies analyst Brent Thill wrote in a note to clients. “For 1Q, the Street is modeling 11% y/y software growth as reported (down from 14% in 4Q), implying a ~3 pt sequential decel on a ~3 pt easier comp and a sharper 22% q/q decel vs -20% in the year-ago period. The earlier-than-expected close of the CFLT deal (mid-March vs. June expectations), combined with prudent Red Hat assumptions (~10% growth), creates room for upward estimate revisions. The broader 1Q setup appears manageable. Cons embeds 8% infrastructure growth (vs. 21% in 4Q) reflecting z17 cyclical spend normalization, alongside 4% consulting growth (vs. 3% in 4Q). Taken together, this implies ~7.4% total revenue growth y/y and a ~13% FCF margin (~$2B).”
  • Thill has a Buy rating on IBM, but lowered his price target to $320 from $370 ahead of the results.

data.title: Novo, Lilly drop as insurers are wary of joining Medicare GLP-1 coverage plan
data.description:
Obesity drugmakers Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly (LLY) traded lower on Tuesday after Bloomberg News reported that leading health insurers remain undecided on whether to join the Trump administration's plans to cover weight loss drugs for Medicare beneficiaries next year.

The insurers had until April 20 to inform the government about their participation in the coverage model known as Better Approaches to Lifestyle and Nutrition for Comprehensive hEalth (BALANCE), announced in December to lower the costs of GLP-1 drugs.

The owner of the Aetna health insurer, CVS Health (CVS), said it has decided not to join the program, a spokesperson for the company said. UnitedHealth (UNH), the largest Medicare Advantage insurer, raised concerns about the model.

“There are some notable challenges and outstanding questions with the currently planned structure,” UnitedHealth’s (UNH) chief of government programs Bobby Hunter remarked at the Q1 earnings call on Tuesday.

However, the company didn’t specifically indicate whether it has opted not to join the program, which requires backing from health insurers representing at least 80% of Medicare Part D members for its implementation on January 1, ⁠2027.

When queried about their participation, other leading insurers, including Humana (HUM), Elevance Health (ELV), and Centene (CNC), didn’t respond or declined to comment.

The program, which leverages lower prices negotiated with drugmakers to expand coverage for GLP-1 medicines, including Lilly’s (LLY) Zepbound and Novo’s (NVO) Wegovy for Medicare D members, would draw pushback from health insurers, a recent study published in JAMA indicated.

data.title: Warsh hearing signals market-friendly Fed ahead - Pepperstone
data.description:
Kevin Warsh’s Federal Reserve Chair  nomination hearing Tuesday is providing investors with clearer signals about how he plans to lead the central bank, with one analyst predicting a shift that could benefit risk assets (SP500).

According to Michael Brown, senior research strategist at Pepperstone, a Warsh-led Fed would mark several key departures from current practice. Interest rates (US2Y) (US10Y) would return as the primary policy tool, while the balance sheet takes a secondary role and potentially shrinks over time within an “ample reserves” framework.

The hearing also suggested the Fed would become notably quieter under Warsh’s leadership. Officials would likely speak less publicly about the rate path and economic outlook. Explicit forward guidance and pre-commitments to policy action would become rare.

Perhaps most significantly for market watchers, the Summary of Economic Projections could undergo changes in its composition or frequency, with the closely followed “dot plot” likely to be eliminated—a move Brown expects would please most market participants.

Brown characterized a Warsh Fed as one that would remain friendly for risk assets, citing an increasing focus on the supply side of the economy and expectations for lower interest rates amid anticipated productivity gains from artificial intelligence (AIQ) (AIEQ).

However, the transition could bring short-term volatility (VIX) as participants adjust to reduced visibility from fewer speeches, forecasts and guidance.

For traders, Brown suggested a risk parity approach could work well, potentially through long positions in S&P 500 futures (SPX) paired with long front-end Treasuries or short-term interest rate packs, with added long rates volatility exposure.
